name: Midway Area (Lavic Jasper)
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding site known for red and brown Lavic jasper specimens. Flat desert terrain with easy access and no permits required - ideal for casual collectors.
description:
The Midway Area, located in the Mojave Desert region of San Bernardino County, is a popular public rockhounding destination famous for its distinctive Lavic jasper deposits. This BLM land offers free, unrestricted rockhounding for personal use with no permits or fees required. The site sits at a low elevation of 1,746 feet in relatively flat terrain, making it accessible to collectors of all experience levels.

Lavic jasper here typically displays rich red, burgundy, brown, and occasionally banded patterns. Specimens range from small thumb-sized pieces to larger chunks suitable for cabochon cutting or display. The jasper occurs in association with chalcedony and agate, often found scattered across the surface and in small washes. Collectors frequently find material suitable for cutting, polishing, and jewelry-making.

The Mojave Desert geology in this region reflects ancient volcanic activity and silica-rich groundwater percolation through rhyolitic volcanic rocks. The Lavic Jasper specifically formed from microcrystalline quartz precipitated in fractures and cavities within the volcanic host rock, typically during the late Tertiary period. Surface weathering and erosion have exposed these deposits over thousands of years, making hand collecting straightforward.

Expect wide open desert landscape with sparse creosote bush and scattered rocks. The flat topography means minimal elevation gain, though the exposed terrain offers limited shade. Most productive collecting occurs along natural washes where erosion concentrates heavier jasper pieces. Rock hammers and chisels help extract specimens from outcrop, though abundant surface material requires no tools.

This location is best visited October through April when temperatures are moderate. Summer heat in the Mojave often exceeds 100 degrees Fahrenheit, making midday collecting uncomfortable. The site works equally well as a half-day or full-day trip depending on your energy level and collecting goals.
history: The Lavic Jasper deposits formed during the late Tertiary volcanic period when silica-rich fluids percolated through fractured rhyolitic volcanic rocks in the Mojave Desert. This region's geology reflects basin-and-range extension and volcanism that created favorable conditions for microcrystalline quartz precipitation, resulting in the colorful jasper bodies found here today. Desert prospectors and rockhounds have collected from this area for decades, making it one of Southern California's most accessible and productive jasper localities.
tips: Search along the natural washes and bajada fans where erosion concentrates heavier jasper specimens; red and burgundy material is most common but watch for banded and variegated pieces. Bring a rock hammer and chisel if you want to extract material from outcrop, but abundant surface specimens require only hand collection. Look for specimens with smooth surfaces and rich color saturation - these are better candidates for lapidary work. The flat terrain lets you cover substantial ground, so plan to spend 3-4 hours exploring different areas of the site to find premium material.
terrain_notes: Flat desert terrain at 1,746 feet elevation with minimal slope. Sparse creosote bush vegetation and scattered volcanic rock. Unpaved dirt roads lead to parking areas near collecting zones. Walking distances from parking typically 0.25 to 1 mile across relatively even ground with loose rocks and minor wash crossings. Footing is generally stable but watch for uneven terrain and sharp volcanic fragments.
best_season: October - April. Summer temperatures in the Mojave Desert regularly exceed 100 degrees Fahrenheit (38 Celsius), making daytime collecting dangerous. Winter months offer ideal conditions with moderate temperatures, clear skies, and comfortable working conditions.
written_directions: From San Bernardino, take Interstate 15 north toward Barstow for approximately 45 miles. Exit near the town of Daggett and head east on Lavic Lake Road or similar service roads accessing BLM land in the Midway Area. GPS coordinates 35.032473, -116.471429 pinpoint the primary collecting zone. High-clearance vehicles are recommended but passenger cars may access main pullouts. Contact the Barstow BLM Field Office for current road conditions before traveling.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt roads in variable condition. Passenger vehicles can access main areas, but recent rain or flash flooding may temporarily close access. Check BLM website for current conditions.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas along service roads. No developed parking lot - park along roadsides away from active collection areas. Capacity varies but typically accommodates 5-15 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or digging equipment. No explosives. Collect only what you can carry. Leave the site in good condition and pack out all trash.
rules:

Responsible collecting practices required
nearest_services: Daggett and Barstow, approximately 20-30 miles west. Both offer gas stations, food, water, and emergency services. Barstow is the larger town with full amenities including rockhounding supply shops.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved dirt roads to parking areas may be rough but passable to standard vehicles. No formal wheelchair access, but the flat ground and open landscape present fewer obstacles than typical desert sites. Sturdy footwear recommended due to sharp volcanic fragments and loose rock. Limited shade available - bring sun protection.

faq.question: What types of jasper can you find at Midway Area near Barstow?
faq.answer: The Midway Area is famous for Lavic jasper, which displays rich red, burgundy, brown, and banded color patterns. You will also find chalcedony and agate specimens. Red and burgundy material is most abundant, with pieces ranging from small thumb-sized specimens to larger chunks suitable for cutting and polishing. The best specimens show solid color saturation and smooth surfaces ideal for lapidary work.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at the Midway Area in San Bernardino County?
faq.answer: No permit is required. The Midway Area sits on BLM public land where personal rockhounding is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. You can collect free of charge with no paperwork - just follow the 25-pound daily limit and personal-use-only rules. This makes it one of Southern California's most accessible free rockhounding sites.

faq.question: How far is the Midway Area from San Bernardino and what is the best route?
faq.answer: The Midway Area is approximately 45-50 miles from San Bernardino. Take Interstate 15 north toward Barstow for about 45 miles, then exit near Daggett. From there, access BLM land via Lavic Lake Road and related service roads. The drive takes about 1.5 to 2 hours depending on traffic. Barstow is the nearest town with full services including gas, food, and emergency supplies.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding the Lavic Jasper deposits?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Mojave Desert summers are extremely hot - temperatures regularly exceed 100 degrees Fahrenheit, making daytime collecting dangerous and uncomfortable. Winter and spring months offer moderate temperatures, clear skies, and pleasant working conditions. Early morning or late afternoon collecting extends your comfort even in shoulder seasons.

faq.question: Can beginners and families collect jasper at the Midway Area rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Absolutely. The Midway Area is beginner and family-friendly. The flat terrain requires minimal hiking, abundant surface material needs no digging equipment, and the site offers straightforward hand collecting. Children as young as 6 can participate. Abundant specimens mean even first-time collectors find quality jasper. No special skills, experience, or tools are required - just bring sunscreen, water, and a bag.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should you bring when rockhounding for Lavic jasper?
faq.answer: For surface collecting, you only need a bag and sunscreen. To extract material from outcrop, bring a rock hammer and cold chisel. Always pack plenty of water (2-3 liters minimum), sun protection, sturdy closed-toe boots, and a hat. The exposed desert offers no shade, so plan accordingly. A GPS unit or smartphone helps you return to productive areas. Since it is remote BLM land, tell someone where you are going.

meta_title: Midway Area Lavic Jasper Rockhounding - California
